6. Item-specific knowledge: within-child, within-word linkage

peekbank-items

Notebook 5 showed that most of the trial-level predictive signal is a child general-ability main effect: the 2AFC contrast (H3) added nothing, and the precise (item-level) pathway did not beat the imputed one. That is the expected result if the only thing being measured is “smarter kids look more at targets.”

Here we ask the sharper question the prior literature never isolated:

Controlling for a child’s general ability and for a word’s general difficulty/salience, does knowing this specific word predict accuracy on this specific trial?

The design that isolates this is a crossed random-effects model. With a child random intercept (absorbing general ability) and target/distractor word random intercepts (absorbing general word difficulty and salience), a produces fixed effect is identified only from within-child, within-word variation — the same child knowing word A but not word B, and the same word known by child X but not child Y. That coefficient is the item-specific knowledge effect.

A key conceptual point falls out immediately: the imputed knowledge signal P(knows) = 2PL(theta_child, a_word, b_word) is a deterministic function of child ability and word difficulty, so once child and word effects are in the model it carries no additional information. Only real item-level CDI (the precise subset) has residual item-specific variance. This notebook is therefore necessarily about the precise subset.

Code 
library(here)
library(lme4)
source(here("helper", "common.R"))
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE)

d_trial <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "1_d_trial.Rds")) |> ungroup()
d_sub   <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "1_d_sub.Rds"))
cdi     <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "3_cdi_difficulty.Rds"))
subset_dat  <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "0_cdi_items_subset.Rds"))
subject_map <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "0_subject_map.Rds"))

p2pl <- function(theta, a, b) plogis(a * (theta - b))

Build the precise frame

Same construction as notebook 5: trial elogit, IRT params for target/distractor, child ability theta, and the item-level produces (nearest-age CDI, bridged on lab_subject_id).

Code 
cdi_diff <- cdi$item_pars |> select(item, a, b)

base <- d_trial |>
  rename(accuracy = long_window_accuracy, elogit = long_window_elogit) |>
  filter(!is.na(elogit)) |>
  mutate(log_age = log(age), log_age_centered = log_age - mean(log_age)) |>
  left_join(cdi_diff |> rename(a_t = a, b_t = b), by = c("target_label" = "item")) |>
  left_join(cdi_diff |> rename(a_d = a, b_d = b), by = c("distractor_label" = "item")) |>
  left_join(d_sub |> select(administration_id, prod), by = "administration_id") |>
  mutate(theta = cdi$prop_to_theta(prod))

items_long <- subset_dat$cdi_items_long |>
  mutate(item = str_to_lower(item), lab_subject_id = as.character(lab_subject_id)) |>
  rename(cdi_age = age)

admins <- base |>
  distinct(administration_id, subject_id, age) |>
  inner_join(subject_map, by = "subject_id") |>
  filter(lab_subject_id %in% items_long$lab_subject_id)

nearest_cdi <- admins |>
  inner_join(items_long |> distinct(lab_subject_id, cdi_age),
             by = "lab_subject_id", relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  mutate(age_diff = abs(age - cdi_age)) |>
  group_by(administration_id) |>
  slice_min(age_diff, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(administration_id, lab_subject_id, cdi_age)

produces_at_age <- items_long |>
  group_by(lab_subject_id, cdi_age, item) |>
  summarise(produces = as.integer(any(produces == 1)), .groups = "drop")

precise <- base |>
  inner_join(nearest_cdi, by = "administration_id") |>
  left_join(produces_at_age |> rename(prec_k_t = produces),
            by = c("lab_subject_id", "cdi_age", "target_label" = "item")) |>
  left_join(produces_at_age |> rename(prec_k_d = produces),
            by = c("lab_subject_id", "cdi_age", "distractor_label" = "item")) |>
  filter(!is.na(prec_k_t), !is.na(prec_k_d))

cat(nrow(precise), "trials,", n_distinct(precise$lab_subject_id), "children,",
    n_distinct(precise$target_label), "target words\n")
25850 trials, 229 children, 22 target words

A. There IS item-specific variance to find

Decompose the real item-level knowledge into the part predictable from child ability × word difficulty (what imputation captures) and the residual (what imputation cannot capture). If the residual variance is ~0, item-level CDI buys nothing; if it is substantial, there is item-specific signal to test.

Code 
res <- precise |>
  filter(!is.na(theta), !is.na(a_t), !is.na(b_t)) |>
  mutate(expected_t = p2pl(theta, a_t, b_t),
         residual_t = prec_k_t - expected_t)

tibble(
  quantity = c("Var(actual produces_target)",
               "Var(expected from ability x difficulty)",
               "Var(residual = item-specific)",
               "share item-specific"),
  value = c(var(res$prec_k_t),
            var(res$expected_t),
            var(res$residual_t),
            var(res$residual_t) / var(res$prec_k_t))
) |> kable(digits = 3)
quantity value
Var(actual produces_target) 0.206
Var(expected from ability x difficulty) 0.093
Var(residual = item-specific) 0.137
share item-specific 0.665

A large residual share means children’s word-by-word knowledge departs substantially from what their overall vocabulary size predicts — exactly the signal a child×item linkage needs.

B. The within-child, within-word test

Nested crossed random-effects models. Child (administration_id) and word (target_label, distractor_label) random intercepts absorb general ability and general word difficulty/salience; the produces fixed effects are then the item-specific knowledge effects.

Code 
ctrl <- lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")
re <- "(1 | administration_id) + (1 | dataset_name) + (1 | target_label) + (1 | distractor_label)"

m0 <- lmer(as.formula(paste("elogit ~ log_age_centered +", re)),
           data = precise, control = ctrl)
m1 <- lmer(as.formula(paste("elogit ~ log_age_centered + prec_k_t +", re)),
           data = precise, control = ctrl)
m2 <- lmer(as.formula(paste("elogit ~ log_age_centered + prec_k_t + prec_k_d +", re)),
           data = precise, control = ctrl)

anova(m0, m1, m2) |> kable(digits = c(0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 0, 4),
  caption = "Does item-specific target/distractor knowledge improve fit?")
Does item-specific target/distractor knowledge improve fit?
npar AIC BIC logLik -2*log(L) Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m0 7 120446.2 120503.3 -60216.1 120432.2 NA NA NA
m1 8 120403.2 120468.5 -60193.6 120387.2 45 1 0
m2 9 120405.1 120478.5 -60193.5 120387.1 0 1 1

Fixed-effect estimates from the full model (these are the within-child, within-word item-specific effects):

Code 
ci <- confint(m2, parm = c("prec_k_t", "prec_k_d"), method = "Wald")
fe <- fixef(m2)[c("prec_k_t", "prec_k_d")]
tibble(
  term = c("knows target (prec_k_t)", "knows distractor (prec_k_d)"),
  estimate = fe,
  ci_low = ci[, 1],
  ci_high = ci[, 2]
) |> kable(digits = 3,
  caption = "Item-specific knowledge effects on trial elogit (target should be +, distractor -)")
Item-specific knowledge effects on trial elogit (target should be +, distractor -)
term estimate ci_low ci_high
knows target (prec_k_t) 0.277 0.194 0.360
knows distractor (prec_k_d) -0.014 -0.097 0.069

C. Descriptive: within-child known vs unknown

The cleanest picture: within each child, compare mean accuracy on trials whose target word they did vs did not produce on the CDI. Differencing within child removes general ability entirely.

Code 
wc <- precise |>
  group_by(lab_subject_id, prec_k_t) |>
  summarise(acc = mean(accuracy), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(knows_target = ifelse(prec_k_t == 1, "knows", "does not know")) |>
  filter(n >= 3)  # need a few trials per cell

wc_paired <- wc |>
  select(lab_subject_id, knows_target, acc) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = knows_target, values_from = acc) |>
  filter(!is.na(knows), !is.na(`does not know`)) |>
  mutate(diff = knows - `does not know`)

ggplot(wc_paired, aes(x = `does not know`, y = knows)) +
  geom_abline(lty = 2) +
  geom_point(alpha = .5) +
  coord_equal() +
  xlab("Accuracy on UNKNOWN target words") +
  ylab("Accuracy on KNOWN target words") +
  labs(subtitle = sprintf(
    "%d children with both cells; mean within-child advantage = %.3f (paired t p = %.4f)",
    nrow(wc_paired), mean(wc_paired$diff),
    t.test(wc_paired$knows, wc_paired$`does not know`, paired = TRUE)$p.value))

Read

The notebook 5 result said the aggregate predictive signal is dominated by general ability. This notebook asks the residual question that actually defines an item-level linkage. Three things to read together:

  • A quantifies whether real item-level CDI even contains item-specific variance beyond what vocabulary size implies (it must, for any of this to work — and it bounds the ceiling for the imputed pathway, which has none).
  • B is the inferential test: a positive prec_k_t (and negative prec_k_d) with the child and word effects partialled out is direct evidence that knowing the specific words drives the specific trial — the 2AFC decision the background analyses argued was being missed.
  • C is the assumption-light version of the same test.

If B/C are positive, the linkage does exist at the item level once measured properly — it was just swamped by ability and salience in every aggregate analysis. If they are null even here, the pessimistic prior conclusion holds even with gold-standard item-level CDI.
